thresholds.status: Currently ~22%; WHO target is 30% reduction; FCTC implementation uneven
Currently ~25 countries; Australia 2012, UK 2016, France 2017, Canada 2019
FDA proposal stalled; political opposition from tobacco-state legislators

thresholds.domain: First WHO FCTC Article 6 fiscal measures (major tobacco markets)
thresholds.status: Philippines, Bangladesh, Philippines have implemented >75% tax share; U.S. at 44%

thresholds.domain: Philip Morris v. Uruguay (plain packaging precedent)
thresholds.status: ICSID upheld Uruguay's plain packaging 2016 — established legal feasibility

items.name: Manufactured scientific uncertainty
items.description: Tobacco companies spent decades funding product-defense science to manufacture doubt about smoking-cancer causation — a documented historical strategy that delayed regulation by 20-30 years and is the paradigm for all subsequent doubt-manufacturing campaigns.

items.name: Nicotine dependency architecture
items.description: Tobacco products are designed to maximize nicotine delivery and maintain dependency; companies manipulated nicotine delivery, pH, and additives specifically to prevent cessation, externalizing the cost of dependency onto users and health systems.

items.name: Regulatory preemption through litigation
items.description: Tobacco companies routinely file trade arbitration claims against countries implementing FCTC measures (Philip Morris v. Uruguay, Philip Morris v. Australia), threatening regulatory chill in lower-income countries unable to bear litigation costs.

methods.welfare_function: W computed via VSL-weighted premature mortality (8M deaths/year at WHO global average VSL), direct healthcare costs (WHO estimates), secondhand smoke harm (global burden of disease), youth initiation lifetime harm, and environmental costs (cigarette butts, agricultural land use).
methods.cooperative_baseline: Nicotine market limited to harm-minimization products (NRT, approved e-cigarettes) with zero youth initiation and registered adult-only access, generating $280B in legitimate nicotine delivery value at fraction of current harm.
key_sources: WHO, Report on the Global Tobacco Epidemic (2023)
ICSID, Philip Morris v. Uruguay Award (2016)
falsification: F1: Demonstrate that Uruguay's comprehensive FCTC MPOWER implementation failed to reduce adult smoking prevalence, contradicting WHO and PAHO monitoring data.
F2: Show that tobacco companies did not manipulate nicotine delivery and pH to maximize dependency, contradicting the 2006 U.S. v. Philip Morris trial findings.
F3: Demonstrate that the tobacco-cancer causal relationship was not known within the industry prior to 1964, contradicting the tobacco company internal document archive.
